title: Choropleth Maps
text: This page maps state-level educational attainment using data from the U.S. Census Bureau ACS 2024 S1501 Educational Attainment table. I focus on the percent of adults age 25 and older with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Choropleth Maps
text: This page maps state-level educational attainment using data from the U.S. Census Bureau ACS 2024 S1501 Educational Attainment table. I focus on the percent of adults age 25 and older with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Choropleth Maps
text:
Data Sources
Data come from the U.S. Census Bureau ACS 2024 S1501 Educational Attainment table. Static map boundaries come from the maps package, and interactive boundaries come from the Leaflet US states GeoJSON file.

title: Choropleth Maps
text:
Numeric Variable: Bachelor’s Degree Attainment

Static Choropleth Map











Interactive Choropleth Map






These maps shows how bachelor’s degree attainment varies across states. Darker states have higher percentages of adults age 25 and older with a bachelor’s degree or higher. The map helps reveal regional differences in educational attainment, showing that in general higher bachelor’s degree attainment is seen in the northeast and west coast, and lower bachelor’s degree attainment is seen in the southeast. West Virginia has the lowest amount of bachelor degrees at 24.4%, and Massachusetts is the sate with the highest amount of bachelor degrees at 48.3% of adults age 25 and older with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Choropleth Maps
text:
Categorical Variable: Education Attainment Categories

Static Choropleth Map











Interactive Choropleth Map






These maps groups states into broader education categories based on the percent of adults age 25 and older with a bachelor’s degree or higher. Compared with the numeric map, the categorical version makes larger regional patterns easier to see because it emphasizes broad group differences rather than small percentage-point changes. In general, higher bachelor’s degree attainment is seen in the northeast of the United States, as well as Washington and Colorado. Bachelor’s degree attainment under 30% is generally seen in the southeast region, as well as Nevada, Kentucky, and West Virginia.

title: Mary Hendrickson

title: Mini Project 1
text: This project analyzes tweets from Donald Trump’s time in office using text mining, regular expressions, and sentiment analysis. The analysis focuses on common words, repeated phrases, emotional language, and changes in tweet style over time.

title: Mini Project 1
text: This project analyzes tweets from Donald Trump’s time in office using text mining, regular expressions, and sentiment analysis. The analysis focuses on common words, repeated phrases, emotional language, and changes in tweet style over time.

title: Mini Project 1
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis
One word that frequently appears in Trump’s tweets is “fake,” typically used to criticize the media outlets or elections.


Code
tidy_tweet |>
  anti_join(smart_stopwords, by = "word") |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 20) |> 
  ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(word, n), n)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
    labs(
    x = "Word",
    y = "Frequency",
    title = "Most Common Words in Trump's Tweets", 
    subtitle = "Top 20 words after removing stopwords"
  )









This is a horizontal bar chart displaying the 20 most common words in trump tweets. On the x axis is the number of times the word has appeared ranging from 0 to 3000, and on the y axis is the 15 most common words. Words are ordered by frequency, with the most frequent words appearing at the top. Most of the words center the U.S and politics, including words relating to issues like border, election, fake, news, and Biden.
The most common words in Trump’s tweets reveals the main topics typically involves politics, elections, and the media.


Code
#counting the frequency of specific sentiments, looking at most common words in anger sentiment.
get_sentiments("nrc") |> 
  filter(sentiment == "anger") |>
  inner_join(tidy_tweet) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10)


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`


# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word          n
   <chr>     <int>
 1 vote        979
 2 bad         573
 3 crime       545
 4 collusion   409
 5 court       404
 6 witch       401
 7 money       354
 8 illegal     327
 9 hoax        297
10 fight       265


Looking at the frequency of words that is categorized in the “anger” sentiment. Many of these expressions of anger seem to relate to political controversies regarding elections or investigations.


Code
tidy_tweet |>
  inner_join(bing_sentiments) |>
  count(sentiment, word, sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(word, n), y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
    geom_col() +  
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~ sentiment, scales = "free") +
  labs(
      title = "Most Common Positive and Negative Words in Trump's Tweets",
      subtitle = "Top 10 words by frequency using the Bing sentiment lexicon",
      x = "Word",
      y = "Frequency",
      fill = "Sentiment"
    )


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









This faceted horizontal bar chart shows the top ten positive and negative words used in Trump’s tweets based on the Bing sentiment lexicon. The x-axis represents word frequency, ranging from 0 to about 1250 for negative words and up to around 3500 for positive words, while the y-axis lists words ordered from most to least frequent. The most common positive words include “great,” “win,” and “strong,” while the most frequent negative words include “fake,” “bad,” and “crime.” These patterns suggest that Trump’s tweets often rely on strongly evaluative language, praising allies or achievements while criticizing opponents. The prominence of the word “fake” also reinforces earlier findings that Trump frequently uses this term to challenge media coverage or political claims.

title: Mini Project 1
